narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 46.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. Looking at your profile, you ranked in the 3rd decile for grades in both your first and third years of law school—well below the typical student's 6th decile—along with an LSAT score of 32 compared to the average 37, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.8 versus the usual 3.2. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, you're male, white, and planning full-time studies, which are all fairly common among applicants. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your third-year decile rank, as being in the lower third pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Your LSAT score was the next biggest influence, with its below-average result also nudging the model toward expecting a fail. Coming in third was your first-year decile, similarly low and contributing to the risk assessment in the same direction. Your family income level, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status followed in importance, each playing a smaller but still failure-leaning role compared to stronger applicants. Overall, your academic performance in law school years stands out as weaker than most students who typically pass the bar, putting you below the middle of the pack where success is more common. While demographics like gender and race didn't sway things much here, they aligned with majority patterns that didn't help offset your grades. The good news is you can take realistic steps to boost your bar exam odds, like dedicating focused time to targeted prep courses that build on your existing knowledge, since your past grades suggest room for stronger study habits. Retaking the LSAT for a future application could also help if you aim to reapply elsewhere, as even a modest score bump makes a big difference. In the meantime, seeking mentorship from upper-decile peers or tutors familiar with bar pitfalls could address the patterns in your school performance. With intentional effort on these fronts, students like you often turn predictions around and succeed. Keep pushing—you've got this potential.
